section.title: I Know That My Redeemer Lives
section.sectionRationale: This chapter holds the highest peak of hope in the entire book. Job describes feeling utterly abandoned, by God, by his family, by his friends, and then, without warning, breaks into the boldest declaration of faith in all his speeches: he knows his Redeemer lives, and will one day stand on the earth, and that Job himself will see God with his own eyes. It is a hope reached for in the dark, not after the dark has lifted.
section.thesis: Job's isolation is now total, mocked by strangers and abandoned by his own family, yet in the depth of that isolation he reaches his highest confession of hope: a living Redeemer who will one day stand on the earth and before whom Job himself will stand, vindicated, in his own flesh.
section.frameNote: Six movements: Job's renewed rebuke of his friends for tormenting him without cause; his complaint that God has besieged him like an enemy in war; his lament that even his family and closest friends now treat him as a stranger; his raw plea for their pity, since the hand of God has struck him; his climactic declaration, I know that my Redeemer lives, and I will see God in my flesh; and his closing warning that his accusers should themselves fear judgment.

units.label: How long will you torment me?

verses.text: Then Job answered:
"How long will you torment me and crush me with your words?
Ten times now you have reproached me; you shamelessly mistreat me.
Even if I have truly gone astray, my error concerns me alone.
If indeed you would exalt yourselves above me and use my disgrace against me,
then understand that it is God who has wronged me and drawn His net around me.

units.label: God has wronged me
units.note: He has veiled my paths with darkness

verses.text: "Though I cry out, 'Violence!' I get no response; though I call for help, there is no justice.
He has blocked my way so I cannot pass; He has veiled my paths with darkness.
He has stripped me of my honor and removed the crown from my head.
He tears me down on every side until I am gone; He uproots my hope like a tree.
His anger burns against me, and He counts me among His enemies.

His troops advance together; they construct a ramp against me 
and encamp around my tent.

units.label: My own family has turned against me
units.note: I am loathsome to my own family

verses.text: "He has removed my brothers from me; my acquaintances have abandoned me.
My kinsmen have failed me, and my friends have forgotten me.
My guests and maidservants count me as a stranger; I am a foreigner in their sight.
I call for my servant, but he does not answer, though I implore him with my own mouth.
My breath is repulsive to my wife, and I am loathsome to my own family.
Even little boys scorn me; when I appear, they deride me.
All my best friends despise me, and those I love have turned against me.
My skin and flesh cling to my bones; I have escaped by the skin of my teeth.

units.label: Have pity on me, my friends
units.note: the hand of God has struck me

verses.text: "Have pity on me, my friends, have pity, for the hand of God has struck me.
Why do you persecute me as God does? Will you never get enough of my flesh?

units.label: I know that my Redeemer lives
units.note: in my flesh I will see God

verses.text: "I wish that my words were recorded and inscribed in a book,
by an iron stylus on lead, or {{chaqaq|chiseled}} in stone forever.
But I know that my {{goel|Redeemer}} lives, and in the end He will stand upon the earth.
Even after my skin has been destroyed, yet in my {{basar|flesh}} I will see God.

I will see Him for myself; my eyes will behold Him, and not as a stranger. 
How my heart {{kalah|yearns}} within me!

units.label: You should fear the sword yourselves

verses.text: "If you say, 'Let us persecute him, since the root of the matter lies with him,'

then you should fear the sword yourselves, because wrath brings punishment by the sword, 
so that you may know there is a judgment."

chaqaq.gloss: To engrave, inscribe, cut permanently into stone or metal. Job wishes his words could be preserved this way forever, a longing that, remarkably, has been fulfilled: this very speech has in fact been engraved in Scripture and read for millennia, outliving every one of the friends who mocked him.

goel.gloss: Redeemer, kinsman-redeemer, one with the right and duty to rescue, avenge, or buy back a relative in distress, as in the book of Ruth. Job's declaration that his go'el lives and will stand on the earth is one of the boldest hopes in the Old Testament, reaching for a living vindicator even while surrounded by loss and isolation.

basar.gloss: Flesh, body, the physical substance of a living person. Job insists that even after his skin, his basar, is destroyed, he will still see God in his flesh, a hope that reaches toward bodily vindication rather than a merely spiritual or disembodied comfort.

kalah.gloss: To be consumed, to yearn, to long intensely to the point of exhaustion. Job's closing exclamation, how my heart yearns within me, captures the overwhelming emotional weight of his hope, longing so deeply for that promised sight of God that his whole being is consumed by it.

basic.context: Job's isolation has become total. Not only do his friends torment him, but his own family, servants, and closest companions now treat him as a stranger; even children mock him. He begs his friends simply to have pity, since God's own hand has struck him. Then, without any narrative preparation, Job reaches the highest point of hope in the entire book: I know that my Redeemer lives, and in the end He will stand upon the earth, and even after my body is destroyed, I will see God in my own flesh. He closes by warning his accusers that they, not he, should fear coming judgment.
basic.hermeneutics: This chapter should not be flattened into a simple prediction of Jesus that Job fully understood; Job himself does not have New Testament clarity about the resurrection or the incarnation. What he does have is a genuine, Spirit-given hope in a living, vindicating kinsman-redeemer who will one day stand on the earth on his behalf, and a hope that this vindication will include his own body, not merely his soul. The New Testament reveals with full clarity what Job here reaches for by faith in the dark.
basic.originalLanguages: Job's go'el, kinsman-redeemer, is the same office described in Ruth, one with family right and duty to rescue a relative in distress; Job applies this legal and family category to God Himself. He insists this vindication will happen in his basar, flesh, not apart from his body, and his closing cry uses kalah, to be consumed with yearning, showing the sheer emotional weight this hope carries for him.
basic.typology: Job's living Redeemer who will one day stand upon the earth anticipates Christ, who is both kinsman, taking on human flesh, and Redeemer, purchasing His people at the cost of His own blood. Job's hope of seeing God in his own flesh, even after his body is destroyed, anticipates the doctrine of bodily resurrection that the New Testament will reveal in full light.
basic.christ: Job's cry, I know that my Redeemer lives, names by faith what the gospel names by sight: Jesus Christ, our kinsman by His incarnation and our Redeemer by His death and resurrection, who has indeed stood, and will stand again, upon the earth. Job hoped to see God in his own flesh after his body was destroyed; the resurrection of Christ, the firstfruits of all who sleep, guarantees exactly this hope for every believer, not as a vague spiritual comfort but as bodily, physical vindication.
basic.god: God is both the one Job accuses of wronging him, drawing a net around him, and the one whose living presence Job trusts will ultimately vindicate him. This is the same tension seen throughout the dialogue, held together without resolution: a God experienced as adversary in present suffering and trusted as Redeemer in ultimate outcome.
basic.crossReferences: Ruth 4:1-10 shows the kinsman-redeemer, go'el, rescuing a family in distress, the legal background behind Job's hope; 1 Corinthians 15:20 names Christ the firstfruits of the resurrection of the dead; Revelation 1:17-18 shows the risen Christ standing, alive forevermore, holding the keys of death and Hades.
basic.oneStory: In the depth of total isolation, Job reaches the book's highest peak of hope: a living Redeemer who will stand upon the earth and before whom Job will stand vindicated, in his own flesh. This hope, reached for in darkness rather than after it lifted, finds its full and certain answer in Jesus Christ, our kinsman-Redeemer, risen bodily and coming again to stand upon the earth.

context.literary: This chapter's structure moves from renewed complaint against the friends, through a devastating catalog of total abandonment by everyone Job loves, to its sudden and unprepared climax of hope in verses 25-27, before returning to a closing warning, a pattern of despair interrupted by hope that has recurred with increasing intensity since chapter 9's cry for a mediator and chapter 16's witness in heaven.
context.historical: The go'el, kinsman-redeemer, was a recognized legal office in ancient Israelite family law, responsible for redeeming property, avenging wrongful death, or rescuing a relative from servitude, most vividly seen in the book of Ruth; Job's application of this category to God is a bold act of faith reaching beyond any human redeemer available to him.
context.canonical: This passage stands among the clearest Old Testament anticipations of bodily resurrection and a personal, living redeemer, a hope that will not be fully or clearly revealed again in Scripture until the New Testament's proclamation of Christ's own bodily resurrection.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To voice, at the height of Job's isolation, the boldest hope the book offers: a living Redeemer who will vindicate him bodily, without yet resolving Job's present suffering, showing that hope can be reached for by faith long before its fulfillment is seen.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes Job's specific hope for his own bodily vindication before God; it does not prescribe that every sufferer will receive an identical resolution in this life. What does endure as abiding, prescriptive truth is the reality this hope points to and that the New Testament confirms: a living Redeemer exists, and bodily resurrection is certain for all who are united to Him.

debates.question: How much did Job himself understand about the future incarnation and resurrection of Christ when he spoke these words?

views.label: Job had genuine but limited insight, a Spirit-inspired hope reaching further than his own full understanding
views.case: Job's language is remarkably specific, a living Redeemer standing on the earth, seeing God in his own flesh, yet the New Testament reveals far more than Job could have grasped about how and through whom this would happen.

views.label: Job's words primarily express confidence in some form of vindication, whether in this life or beyond it, without a developed doctrine of bodily resurrection
views.case: The Hebrew of verses 25-27 is notoriously difficult to translate with certainty, and some scholars read Job's hope as expecting vindication either within his lifetime or through a legal witness, rather than a clear statement of resurrection after death.

debates.bearing: Both readings affirm that Job reaches for real hope beyond his present circumstances; Christian readers may rightly see this passage as a genuine, Spirit-given anticipation of Christ and the resurrection without needing to claim Job possessed full New Testament clarity himself.

study.originalLanguages: Job's go'el, kinsman-redeemer, invokes the same family-rescue office described in Ruth, applied here directly to God Himself. He insists his vindication will include his basar, flesh, not merely his soul, and his closing exclamation uses kalah, to be consumed with yearning, showing how completely this hope has taken hold of him even amid total isolation.

typology.type: The living Redeemer who will one day stand upon the earth
typology.fulfillment: Jesus Christ, our kinsman by incarnation and Redeemer by His atoning death, risen and alive forevermore, Revelation 1:18.

typology.type: Job's hope to see God in his own flesh after his body is destroyed
typology.fulfillment: The bodily resurrection of believers, guaranteed by Christ's own resurrection as firstfruits, 1 Corinthians 15:20-23.

study.christ: Job's declaration, I know that my Redeemer lives, names by faith in the dark what the New Testament announces in the full light of the resurrection: Jesus Christ, our kinsman through His incarnation and our Redeemer through His death, alive and coming again to stand upon the earth. Job hoped to see God in his own flesh even after his body was destroyed; the risen Christ, the firstfruits of the resurrection, secures exactly that hope for every believer, guaranteeing not a vague spiritual survival but genuine bodily life beyond the grave.
study.god: God remains, in this chapter, both the one Job accuses of wronging him, besieging him like an enemy, and the one whose future, living vindication Job clings to with his whole heart. The tension is not resolved here; it is held, faithfully, by a sufferer who refuses to let either his complaint or his hope go.

crossReferences.note: The kinsman-redeemer, go'el, rescues a family from distress, the legal and relational background Job draws on to describe his hoped-for divine Redeemer.
Christ the firstfruits of the resurrection of the dead, guaranteeing the bodily vindication Job hopes for in 19:26.
The risen Christ, alive forevermore and holding the keys of death and Hades, the living Redeemer Job's hope anticipates.

study.oneStory: At the very depth of his isolation, abandoned by friends and family alike, Job reaches the book's highest peak of hope: a living Redeemer who will stand upon the earth, before whom Job himself will stand vindicated in his own flesh. This hope, voiced by faith before its fulfillment could be seen, finds its complete and certain answer in Jesus Christ, our kinsman-Redeemer, risen bodily from the dead.

sermon.title: Holding on to Your Faith in the Midst of Suffering: Job, Part 2
sermon.why: Piper devotes an extended section of this message to Job 19:25-27, tracing Job's progress of faith from despair over death in chapter 7 to this climactic confession, showing that Job's Redeemer is God Himself and, from the New Testament's vantage point, ultimately Christ who redeemed him.

quiz.q: How complete is Job's isolation described in 19:13-19?

options.text: Total: his brothers, acquaintances, kinsmen, servants, wife, and even children now treat him as a stranger or mock him.
options.note: This catalog of abandonment, verses 13-19, is one of the most comprehensive pictures of isolation in the Bible.

options.text: Limited only to his three friends, while his family remains supportive.
options.note: The passage specifically describes Job's own family, wife, servants, and children as having turned against him too, not only his friends.

options.text: Job exaggerates for rhetorical effect but is not truly isolated.
options.note: The text presents this catalog as Job's genuine experience within the narrative, not merely rhetorical exaggeration.

options.text: Only his servants have abandoned him.
options.note: The passage names brothers, acquaintances, kinsmen, his wife, and even children, far broader than servants alone.

quiz.q: What does Job declare in his climactic statement in 19:25?

options.text: That he knows his Redeemer lives and will, in the end, stand upon the earth.
options.note: This is the chapter's central and most quoted declaration of hope.

options.text: That he no longer believes in any form of justice.
options.note: This is the opposite of Job's actual declaration, which is a bold statement of hope and trust.

options.text: That his friends will finally repent of their accusations.
options.note: Job's declaration in verse 25 concerns his Redeemer, not a prediction about his friends' future repentance, though he does warn them of coming judgment in verses 28-29.

options.text: That his wealth will be restored within the year.
options.note: No such specific timeline or promise about wealth appears in this declaration.

quiz.q: What does Job hope for regarding his own body in 19:26?

options.text: That even after his skin is destroyed, he will see God in his own flesh.
options.note: This hope reaches toward bodily vindication, not merely a disembodied spiritual comfort.

options.text: That his body will never actually die.
options.note: Job explicitly acknowledges his body will be destroyed; his hope is for what comes after, not for avoiding death entirely.

options.text: That his body will be preserved exactly as it currently is, covered in boils.
options.note: Job's hope is for vindication and seeing God after his body's destruction, not for preservation of his current diseased state.

options.text: That only his soul, not his body, will survive.
options.note: Job specifically hopes to see God in his flesh, indicating a bodily rather than purely disembodied hope.

quiz.q: What is the meaning and background of the term 'Redeemer' (go'el) in 19:25?

options.text: It refers to a kinsman-redeemer, a family member with the legal right and duty to rescue or avenge a relative in distress, a role Job applies here directly to God.
options.note: This legal and family category, seen clearly in the book of Ruth, is the background for Job's bold application of the term to God Himself.

options.text: It refers to a hired legal advocate with no family connection.
options.note: The go'el specifically carries family or kinship obligation, distinct from a hired, unrelated legal advocate.

options.text: It is a term exclusively used for financial transactions with no personal rescue implied.
options.note: While the go'el could redeem property, the term also carries the sense of personal rescue and vindication, which is Job's primary use here.

options.text: It has no meaningful background elsewhere in the Old Testament.
options.note: The term is richly developed elsewhere, especially in the book of Ruth, providing important background for understanding Job's usage here.

quiz.q: How does this chapter's hope connect to the New Testament's teaching about Christ?

options.text: It anticipates Christ as the living Redeemer, both kinsman through His incarnation and Redeemer through His death and resurrection, who guarantees bodily resurrection for believers.
options.note: This connection, drawn out in passages like 1 Corinthians 15:20-23 and Revelation 1:17-18, is one of the clearest typological threads in the book of Job.

options.text: It has no connection to the New Testament's teaching on Christ.
options.note: This passage is widely recognized as one of the Old Testament's clearest anticipations of the hope fulfilled in Christ.

options.text: It is fulfilled completely by Job's earthly restoration in chapter 42.
options.note: While Job is restored in this life by chapter 42, his hope here reaches toward something beyond mere earthly restoration, toward seeing God Himself in resurrected flesh.

options.text: It teaches that redemption is only ever spiritual, never bodily.
options.note: Job's hope explicitly includes his flesh, and the New Testament confirms a fully bodily resurrection, not merely a spiritual one.

questions: Job's hope in a living Redeemer breaks out suddenly, in the middle of total isolation, not after his circumstances improved. Where might you need to voice hope in Christ even while your own circumstances remain unresolved?
Job's own family and closest friends turned against him in his suffering. Have you ever experienced isolation from people you expected to stand by you, and how did you keep trusting God through it?

Job hopes not just for spiritual comfort but to see God in his own flesh, a bodily hope. How does the promise of bodily resurrection, not just 'going to heaven,' shape the way you think about your own future or grieve the loss of others?

Job wished his words could be inscribed in stone forever, and in a sense they have been, preserved in Scripture for thousands of years. What words or convictions do you hold now that you hope will outlast your present circumstances?

Job calls God both his adversary, who has wronged him, and his Redeemer, who lives. How do you hold together your honest complaints to God with your deepest trust in Him, without needing to resolve the tension first?

translationNotes.note: Job's wish that his words be inscribed with an iron stylus on lead, or chiseled in stone, describes ancient methods of creating a permanent, indestructible record, a wish remarkably fulfilled by this speech's permanent place in Scripture.

Redeemer renders go'el, the kinsman-redeemer of Israelite family law, most vividly illustrated in the book of Ruth; Job's application of this family category to God is a bold theological move reaching beyond any human redeemer available to him.

This verse is one of the most textually and grammatically debated in the Old Testament; the BSB's rendering, in my flesh I will see God, reflects the reading that affirms Job's hope of bodily vindication, the interpretation followed by most historic Christian commentary.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Job 19:1-29. No em or en dashes appear in this chapter's BSB text, and none are used anywhere in this file.
